agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "Subscription billing platform managing lifecycle states (activation, renewal, cancellation, trials) with payment validation, retry logic, and tier-specific grace periods.",
    "bounded_context": "Subscription Billing",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Subscription", "definition": "A recurring billing agreement between a customer and the platform, with a defined billing cycle and tier." },
      { "term": "Payment Method", "definition": "A stored instrument (card, bank account) used to collect subscription fees. Has a status of valid or failed." },
      { "term": "Billing Cycle", "definition": "The recurring time period for which a subscription fee is charged." },
      { "term": "Renewal", "definition": "The automated process of charging the subscriber's payment method to extend the subscription for another billing cycle." },
      { "term": "Grace Period", "definition": "A time window after payment failure during which the subscription remains active before cancellation." },
      { "term": "Premium Tier", "definition": "A subscription level that grants enhanced benefits, including a 14-day grace period on failed renewals." },
      { "term": "Free Trial", "definition": "A time-limited subscription at no cost, limited to one active trial per email address." },
      { "term": "Failed Payment Method", "definition": "A payment method that has been declined or is otherwise unable to process charges." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "A subscription cannot be activated with a failed payment method on file.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a state integrity constraint. Activating a subscription with a known-bad payment method creates a corrupt system state — the platform would immediately owe service to a customer it cannot bill. This isn't a policy preference; it's a precondition for a valid subscription to exist. Allowing this would mean the system has made a financial commitment it structurally cannot collect on. No VP would 'relax' this — they'd be authorizing guaranteed revenue loss.",
      "testable_assertion": "GIVEN a payment method with status=failed, WHEN a subscription activation is attempted using that payment method, THEN the activation MUST be rejected and the subscription MUST NOT transition to active state."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "Subscriptions auto-renew 7 days before the billing cycle ends to allow retry time.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "The 7-day lead time is a business policy tuning parameter, not a data integrity constraint. The system would not be in a corrupt state if this were changed to 5 days or 10 days. This is an operational decision balancing retry window length against premature billing. A product or finance team could adjust this threshold based on payment processor SLAs or retry success rate data without violating any fundamental domain truth.",
      "testable_assertion": "GIVEN an active subscription with a billing cycle end date, WHEN the current date is exactly 7 days before that end date, THEN the system MUST initiate an auto-renewal charge attempt."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "After 3 failed renewal attempts, the subscription is automatically cancelled.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "The number 3 is a policy threshold, not a physical or data integrity constraint. The system is not corrupt if the retry count is changed to 2 or 5. This is a business decision balancing customer retention against carrying unpaid accounts. A VP of Revenue could reasonably change this to 4 attempts or 2 attempts based on churn analysis. The concept that 'eventually we must cancel' might be invariant, but the specific count of 3 is clearly policy.",
      "testable_assertion": "GIVEN an active subscription (non-Premium tier) with exactly 3 consecutive failed renewal attempts, WHEN the 3rd failure is recorded, THEN the subscription MUST be automatically transitioned to cancelled state."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "A cancelled subscription cannot be reactivated — customers must create a new subscription.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a state transition integrity constraint. The cancelled state is terminal — it enforces a one-way lifecycle that protects billing history, audit trails, and revenue recognition. Reactivating a cancelled subscription would create ambiguity: does the old billing history carry forward? Do failed payment counts reset? What about prorated charges? Allowing reactivation would corrupt the subscription lifecycle model. New subscriptions ensure a clean billing record and reset all state. This is the kind of constraint where violation means the aggregate's invariants are broken.",
      "testable_assertion": "GIVEN a subscription in cancelled state, WHEN any attempt is made to transition it to active state, THEN the operation MUST be rejected. The only path to active service is creating a new subscription entity."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "Premium tier subscribers get a 14-day grace period before cancellation instead of the 3-strike rule.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "The 14-day duration is a business policy parameter — a retention strategy for high-value customers. The system is not in a corrupt state if this is changed to 10 days or 21 days. This is a commercial decision that balances premium customer retention against unpaid service costs. A product team could adjust this based on premium churn data or competitive pressure. The concept that premium customers get differentiated treatment might persist, but the specific 14-day window is pure policy.",
      "testable_assertion": "GIVEN a Premium tier subscription with a failed renewal, WHEN the failed renewal occurs, THEN the subscription MUST remain active for 14 calendar days. IF payment is not successfully collected within 14 days, THEN the subscription MUST be automatically cancelled."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "Free trial subscriptions cannot have more than one active trial per email address at any time.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a uniqueness constraint protecting system integrity and preventing fraud. One-active-trial-per-email is not a tunable preference — it's a structural guarantee that the trial benefit is consumed once per identity. Violating this means the system has allowed trial abuse, which corrupts the commercial model of trials as a one-time acquisition tool. This is analogous to a unique constraint in the data model. If you allow duplicates, you've broken the fundamental purpose of a trial. A business might later decide to allow a second trial for a different product, but that's a new rule — not relaxing this one.",
      "testable_assertion": "GIVEN an email address that already has an active free trial subscription, WHEN a new free trial subscription creation is attempted for the same email address, THEN the creation MUST be rejected and the existing trial MUST remain unaffected."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [
    {
      "id": "AC-1",
      "given": "A customer attempts to activate a subscription with a payment method whose status is 'failed'",
      "when": "The activation request is submitted",
      "then": "The system rejects the activation, returns a clear error indicating the payment method is invalid, and the subscription remains in an inactive/draft state"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-2",
      "given": "An active subscription with a billing cycle ending on date X",
      "when": "The current date reaches X minus 7 days",
      "then": "The system initiates an auto-renewal charge against the subscriber's payment method on file"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-3",
      "given": "A non-Premium active subscription has accumulated 3 consecutive failed renewal attempts",
      "when": "The 3rd failure is recorded",
      "then": "The subscription is automatically transitioned to cancelled state and the customer is notified"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-4",
      "given": "A subscription is in cancelled state",
      "when": "Any request is made to reactivate or resume the subscription",
      "then": "The system rejects the request and directs the customer to create a new subscription"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-5",
      "given": "A Premium tier subscription experiences a failed renewal",
      "when": "The failure is recorded",
      "then": "The subscription remains active and a 14-day grace period timer begins. Cancellation occurs only if no successful payment is collected within those 14 days"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-6",
      "given": "A customer with an active free trial attempts to create another free trial using the same email address",
      "when": "The trial creation request is submitted",
      "then": "The system rejects the request, citing the existing active trial, and the existing trial remains unaffected"
    }
  ],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Payment method transitions from valid to failed while subscription activation is in progress", "expected_behavior": "The activation must re-validate payment method status at the moment of final commitment. If failed at commit time, reject activation." },
    { "scenario": "Renewal attempt succeeds on the 3rd try (exactly at the boundary)", "expected_behavior": "The subscription remains active. Cancellation only triggers after the 3rd failure, not on the 3rd attempt." },
    { "scenario": "Premium subscriber downgrades to standard tier during the 14-day grace period", "expected_behavior": "Spec is ambiguous — see escalation. Likely the grace period should complete under original terms, but this needs clarification." },
    { "scenario": "Customer has a cancelled trial and attempts a new trial with the same email", "expected_behavior": "The rule says 'active trial' — a cancelled trial is not active, so a new trial should be permitted. However, this may conflict with anti-abuse intent." },
    { "scenario": "Auto-renewal fires 7 days before cycle end, but the 3 retries exhaust before the cycle actually ends", "expected_behavior": "Cancellation should proceed immediately upon the 3rd failure, not wait for the cycle end date." },
    { "scenario": "Premium subscriber's grace period of 14 days overlaps with the next billing cycle start", "expected_behavior": "The grace period governs — no new billing cycle charge should be initiated while the previous cycle's grace period is still active." },
    { "scenario": "Customer has two email addresses and attempts a free trial on each", "expected_behavior": "The rule is per-email, so both trials are permitted. If abuse prevention requires per-person uniqueness, additional identity checks are needed beyond email." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "These rules directly protect revenue integrity. BR-1 prevents unbillable activations. BR-3/BR-5 balance retention against carrying costs of unpaid accounts. The trial uniqueness rule (BR-6) prevents acquisition cost inflation from trial abuse.",
    "operational": "The 7-day renewal lead time (BR-2) creates a retry window that reduces involuntary churn. The Premium grace period (BR-5) adds operational complexity — the system must track two distinct cancellation pathways based on tier. The terminal cancellation state (BR-4) simplifies lifecycle management but requires clear UX for re-subscription.",
    "user": "Customers benefit from retry windows preventing accidental service loss. Premium customers receive meaningfully better treatment, reinforcing tier value. The no-reactivation rule (BR-4) may frustrate customers who expect to 'resume' — clear messaging is critical. Trial limits may frustrate users who feel their trial was wasted.",
    "risk": "Primary risk is around BR-4's strictness — if a billing system error caused cancellation, there's no recovery path short of creating a new subscription (potentially losing grandfathered pricing). BR-5's grace period interaction with tier changes is underspecified and could be exploited or misapplied.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Subscribers (Standard)", "impact": "Subject to 3-strike cancellation with no reactivation. Must ensure payment methods are valid before activation." },
      { "group": "Subscribers (Premium)", "impact": "Receive favorable 14-day grace period, reinforcing premium value proposition." },
      { "group": "Free Trial Users", "impact": "Limited to one active trial per email. May need clearer communication about trial terms." },
      { "group": "Finance / Revenue Team", "impact": "Revenue recognition is cleaner with terminal cancellation states. Grace periods create deferred revenue risk." },
      { "group": "Customer Support", "impact": "Will handle escalations from customers who want to reactivate cancelled subscriptions. Need clear policy and tooling for creating replacement subscriptions." },
      { "group": "Fraud / Trust & Safety", "impact": "Trial uniqueness rule is a first line of defense but email-only enforcement is weak against multi-account abuse." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 62,
    "reasoning": "The core rules are clearly stated and classifiable, but several interaction effects between rules are unspecified. The Premium grace period's interaction with tier changes and with the retry cadence is ambiguous. The trial rule's scope (active only vs. lifetime limit) has anti-abuse implications that aren't addressed. The retry timing within the 7-day window is also unspecified (how spaced are the 3 attempts?).",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "BR-1 classification as invariant — clear state integrity constraint",
      "BR-4 classification as invariant — terminal state transition is structural",
      "BR-6 classification as invariant — uniqueness constraint on trials",
      "BR-2 and BR-3 classification as non-invariant — clearly tunable policy parameters"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Interaction between Premium grace period and tier changes mid-grace",
      "Retry cadence and timing within the 7-day renewal window",
      "Whether 'one active trial per email' is intended as lifetime or concurrent limit",
      "What happens to grandfathered pricing when BR-4 forces new subscription creation"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "Payment method status is a binary valid/failed (no intermediate states like 'expiring soon')",
      "The 3-strike rule for standard tier counts consecutive failures within a single renewal cycle, not across cycles",
      "The 14-day Premium grace period starts from the first failed renewal attempt of the cycle",
      "Email address is the sole identity key for trial uniqueness enforcement"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The spec does not define what happens if a Premium subscriber downgrades to Standard tier during the 14-day grace period. Does the grace period convert to 3-strike logic, or does it complete under original Premium terms?",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Premium Subscribers", "Customer Support", "Finance"],
      "options": ["Grace period completes under original Premium terms (favor customer)", "Immediately switch to 3-strike logic for current cycle (favor consistency)", "Prevent tier changes during grace period entirely"],
      "recommendation": "Complete grace period under original terms — changing rules mid-crisis is hostile to the customer and creates billing edge cases."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The retry cadence within the 7-day renewal window is unspecified. How are the 3 attempts spaced? Daily? Every other day? All on the first day?",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Subscribers (Standard)", "Finance", "Payment Operations"],
      "options": ["Space evenly (day 1, day 3, day 5)", "Escalating intervals (day 1, day 3, day 6)", "Leave to implementation discretion"],
      "recommendation": "Specify explicitly — retry spacing materially affects recovery rates and should be a defined business rule, not an implementation detail."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "BR-6 says 'one active trial per email at any time' — does this mean a customer whose trial expired or was cancelled can start a new trial? The literal reading says yes (it's no longer 'active'), but the anti-abuse intent may require a lifetime limit.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Free Trial Users", "Marketing / Growth", "Fraud / Trust & Safety"],
      "options": ["Concurrent limit only (allow re-trials after previous trial ends)", "Lifetime limit per email (one trial ever)", "Lifetime limit with exceptions for specific campaigns"],
      "recommendation": "Clarify with Product — if this is an acquisition tool, lifetime limit is likely intended. If it's a re-engagement tool, concurrent limit makes sense."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
